activities.markdown:
# The Jazz Guitarist's Chord Workout

This lesson consolidates everything in Unit 14 into a systematic practice regimen.

## Daily Chord Workout (20 minutes)

### Segment 1 — Shell Voicings (5 min)
Cycle through ii-V-I in all 12 keys using only shell voicings (3-7 of each chord).

Key sequence: C → F → Bb → Eb → Ab → Db → F# → B → E → A → D → G

### Segment 2 — Drop-2 Voicings (5 min)
Pick one chord quality (e.g., dominant 7). Play it in all 12 keys staying on strings 5-4-3-2. Then move to strings 4-3-2-1.

### Segment 3 — Voice Leading (5 min)
Pick a ii-V-I progression and find the smoothest possible voice leading between the three chords. Try 3 different voicings for each.

### Segment 4 — Free Application (5 min)
Play over a jazz backing track using only the chords you've learned today. Don't overthink — trust your ears.

## Target Chord Shapes to Know

| Type | Target Quality |
|------|----------------|
| Major | maj7, maj9, maj7#11 |
| Minor | min7, min9, min11 |
| Dominant | 7, 9, 13, 7b9, 7#9, 7b13 |
| Half-dim | m7b5 |
| Diminished | dim7 |

activities.question: Why is it important to practice jazz chord voicings through all 12 keys?
choices: Jazz songs are only written in uncommon keys
To develop automatic, key-independent muscle memory for any real-world chord change
Because only 12-key practice counts for theory exams
activities.explanation: Jazz tunes appear in all 12 keys (Bb, Eb, and Ab are especially common for horn players). Practicing chord voicings through all keys develops automatic, transposable muscle memory — so you can comp any chord in any key without thinking about fingering.

activities.question: What are the three most common altered dominant extensions used in jazz chord comping?
activities.explanation: The most colorful jazz dominant alterations are b9 (one half step above root), #9 (the 'Hendrix chord' augmented 9th), and b13 (lowered 13th, same as #5). These create tension that resolves powerfully to the tonic.
